data.name: SMGW
data.description: A Smart Meter Gateway (SMGW) collects meter data, packages it into InfoReports, and controls household connection devices via ControlMessages. It serves as a secure communication hub between smart meters, home devices, and external systems, enabling efficient data exchange and remote management.

threats.title: Insider Privilege Abuse
threats.description: An insider (authorized user like an empoyee, contractor or third party) can push an update in order to insert either a backdoor or grand privileges to unauthorized people. They can also take control of the network for malicious purposes or personal gain.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the IT-SiG/KRITIS regualtion and the BSI-Grundschutz, which require least privilege implementation through RBAC, continuous monitoring, contractor vetting, seperation of duties and logging. The IEC 62351 mandates secure remote access and strong authentication.
- Least privilege
- Behavioral monitoring
- Strict contractor vetting
- Secure remote access
- Seperation of Duties
- Strong authentication
- Logging and audit trials
- Vendor and contractor vetting
 RBAC

threats.title: Privacy Leakage via Smart Meter Data
threats.description: A third party or otherwise compromised IoT device can leak information about the grid or customer information, leading to a privacy degredation. The severity could be higher, depending on the information that is communicated/collected.
threats.mitigation:
Partially mitigated as IEC 62351 requires TLS 1.2 which is vulnerable to subversion attacks and anonymization is not required to use differential privacy which is the only known anonymization mechanism to ensure privacy.
- Device authentication 
- network segmentation
- privacy-preserving data aggregation
- TLS 1.3
- Anonymization (differential privacy) and aggregation
- Access control
- IDS

data.name: VNB-SCADA
data.description: A VNB-SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) system is a distribution system operator’s control platform for monitoring smart meter data and managing Controllable Local Systems (CLS) through load and generation commands. It provides high-level oversight and control of devices, processes, and distributed control systems to ensure efficient and secure grid operation.

data.name: Command Frame
data.description: A Command Frame is a control message that includes start and stop transmissions, queries about configurations or header information, control commands directed to specific PMUs, and requests for historical PMU data. It enables structured communication and control between the monitoring system and phasor measurement units.

threats.title: Insider Privilege Abuse
threats.description: An insider (authorized user like an empoyee, contractor or third party) can push an update in order to insert either a backdoor or grand privileges to unauthorized people. They can also take control of the network for malicious purposes or personal gain.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351, which require the use of RBAC and at least TLS 1.2. Furthermore, the BSI-Grundschutz in combination with the SiG/KRITIS regulation require the implementation of behavioral monitoring, seperation of duties and vendor vetting.
- Least privilege
- Behavioral monitoring
- Strict contractor vetting
- Secure remote access
- Seperation of Duties
- Strong authentication
- Logging and audit trials
- Vendor and contractor vetting
 RBAC

threats.title: Timestamp Tampering
threats.description: Affecting coordination between protection systems and control centers by altering or manipulating timestamps asccociated with data, commands or logs. This is done to either bypass security controls, hide malicious activity or disrupt synchronization and sequencing in critical systems.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz. Incombination with IEC 61850 it requires authenticated time sources, redundant time synchronization mechanisms and secure time synchronization protocos. In combincation with IEC 62351 it also mandates monitoring of anomalies in time data, file/digital integrity checks and strong authentication. TLS 1.2 for all communiations is also mandated, but leafs room for subversion attacks.
- Use authenticated time sources
- Redundant time synchronization mechanisms
- Monitor for anomalies
- Secure time synchronization protocols (network time protocol (NTP) with authentication or precision time protocol (PTP))
- TLS 1.3
- Strong authentication
- File integrity checks

threats.title: Communication Protocol Downgrade
threats.description: Forcing systems to use weaker communication protocols, for example downgrade from TLS 1.3 to TLS 1.0.
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 with the BSI-Grundschutz mandates the use of secure and up-to-date protocols, disabling legacy/weak protocols and protocol version pinning. Furthermore requires the use of nonces/timestamps to prevent replay and the BSI-Grundschutz requires network segmentation.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Use nonces/timestamps to prevent replay
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support
- Network segmentation
- Protocol version pinning

threats.title: Exploit Vulnerable Protocols
threats.description: Exploiting vulnerabilities in DNP3, GOOSE over IEC 61850, MMS over IEC 61850, and IEC 61850-5-104
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 require the use of secure and up-to-date protocols, however their requirements are not updated frequently enough, such that IEC 62351 for example only requires TLS 1.2 and recommends TLS 1.3 instead of requiring TLS 1.3 to prevent subversion attacks. Otherwise, the two regulations mandate the disabling of legacy/weak protocols, the use of MACs and digital signatures such as HMAC, strong authentication through MFA and continuous monitoring. The BSI-Grundschutz also requires network segmentation and vendor managed software/code singing.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support

threats.title: IoT Botnet Participation
threats.description: An IoT device is compromised and part of a botnet, which leads to ignored control commands, false data injection and/or control sequence injection. They can also be used to send spam, extend the botnet or perform a DoS or DDoS attack.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz in combination with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation require network segmentation, IDS, secure onboarding and secure lifecycle management as well as privacy-preserving data aggregation. Note that the privacy requirements are not optimal. IEC 62351 also requires strong authentication and communication encryption TLS 1.2, which is vulnerable to subversion attacks.
- Device authentication 
- Network segmentation
- Privacy-preserving data aggregation
- Anomaly detection (IDS)
- Secure onboarding and lifecycle
- TLS 1.3

threats.title: Rogue Device Injection
threats.description: Entering a third party device that porpusfully inserts false data or ignores control commands. Furthermore, it can impersonate legitimite equipment, collect sensitive data or be a foothold for further attacks.
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 requires strong authenticaion and communication via TLS 1.2, leaving room for subversion attacks. While the BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation network segmentation, RBAC and privacy-preserving data aggregation regquire. Note that differential privacy is not mandated, so there is room for improvment and a possible attack vector. TR-30109 for SMGW and the BSI-Grundschutz also require CRL.
- Strong authentication (TLS 1.3) 
- Network segmentation
- Privacy-preserving data aggregation
- Role-based access control
- CRL

threats.title: Unauthorized Voltage Setpoint Change
threats.description: Adversaries manipulate voltage setpoints in real-time via compromised micro-controllers, causing equipment damage or cascading outages.
threats.mitigation:
With the BSI-Grundschutz and the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation in use, it is required to use RBAC, anomaly detection via IDS and to have redundancy and fallback in place. With the IEC 62351 it is mandated to use TLS 1.2 for communications (subverion attack vulnerable), command validation (also IEC 61850), disable legacy protocols and use strong authentication (MFA).
- Role-based access control
- Strong Authentication (MFA)
- Command Validation
- Anomaly detection (IDS)
- End-to-End encryption (TLS 1.3)
- Redundancy and fallback
- Disable legacy protocols

threats.title: Command Injection
threats.description: Inserting malicious control commands into legitimate communication streams
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 is the most important regulation with the BSI-Grundschutz, which require MFA, RBAC, input validation, TLS 1.2 (subversion attack vulnerable) and secure boot. IEC 62351 also required parameterized commands to prevent malicious free-from commands.
- Strong authentication (MFA, certificates)
- Role-based access control (RBAC)
- Input validation and whitelisting
- TLS 1.3 and ceritificate pinning (IEC 62351-3)
- Parameterized Commands (structured data formats)
- Secure Boot

threats.title: Replay Attack
threats.description: Replaying legitimate control commands at inappropriate times
threats.mitigation:
The threat is mostly mitigated by IEC 62351, which ensures TLS 1.2 (subversion attack vulnerable), nonce/timestamp usage, authenticated time sources, MACs, and sequence number checking. In combination with the BSI-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation it also requires redundant time synchronization, anomaly detection and monitoring, file/session integrity validation and network segmentation.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Use nonces/timestamps to prevent replay
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support
- Network Segmentation
- Anomaly Detection

threats.title: Man-in-the-Middle
threats.description: Intercepting certificate validation traffic to enable impersonation attacks
threats.mitigation:
Similar to other communication threats this threat is partially mitigated through the BSI-Grundschutz, which enforces regular key rotation, network segmentation, strict usage of secure communication channels (TLS 1.2 & HTTPS [subverions attack vulnerable]) and with TR-03109 for SMGW requires certificate pinning. Most of them are also enforces through the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation.
- Enforce certificate pinning
 - strict TLS validation
- Use HSTS and OCSP stapling
 - Regular Key Rotation
 Network segmentation

threats.title: False Data Injection
threats.description: Manipulating sent sensor readings from smart meters, PMUs, Protected-IEDs and other sources to cause incorrect state estimation, mask faults, trigger false control actions and evade anomaly detection.
threats.mitigation:
For this the IEC 62351 and BSI-Grundschutz are the most important regulations. They require the use of TLS 1.2 (subversion attack), sigital signatues via CMS ond all data flows, anonmaliy detection and cross-validation (also IEC 61850) and in combination with IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation network segmentation and redundant measurements. The BSI-Grundschutz also requires privacy-preserving data aggregation, which however does not need to use differential privacy and thus leafs room for attack vectors.
- End-to-end encryption (TLS 1.3) and digital signatures on all data flows (CMS)
- Anomaly detection and cross-validation with redundant data sources
 - Redundant measurements
 - network isolation

threats.title: Backdoored Firmware
threats.description: Backdoored firmware in field devices (e.g., smart meters or protection relays) enables persistent access.
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 requires the use of secure boot, code signing and verification, as well es limited access priviledges, which is enforces by the BSI-Grundschutz. This also mandates with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation to utilize vendor risk assesment, zero trust supply chain model and regular patching and updating.
- Vendor Risk Assesment
- Code Signing & Verification (CMS)
- Secure Boot
- Ongoing Code Scanning
- Zero Trust Supply Chain Model
- Limited Access Privileges
- Regular Patching/Updating

data.name: Aggregated InfoReports
data.description: Aggregated InfoReports consist of a collected batch of InfoReports sent by the SCADA system from multiple Smart Meter Gateways (SMGWs) to the Distribution System Operator's Distribution Management System (DSO-DMS). These reports contain important telemetry data transmitted in batches, supporting periodic monitoring and data forwarding without real-time requirements.

threats.title: SCADA Operational Data
threats.description:
Altering operational data flowing to DSO-DMS systems, to manipulate grid operations, evade detection, cuase physical damage and/or mask faults.
Mitigated by message integrity and authentication which is enforced by TLS 1.2/3 and IEC 62351.
threats.mitigation:
THe BSI-Grundschtz requires network segmentation, strict firewall rules, continuous monitoring and incident response planning. It also requires RBAC and IEC 62351 mandates the use of validation with redundant sources, digital signatures via CMS, TLS 1.2 (subversion attack vulnerable). These are also mainly enforced through the IT-SiG/KRITIS reggulation.
- Network segmentation between IT and OT
- Strict firewall rules
- Validate with redundant sources
 - Digital Signatures (CMS)
- Connection via TLS 1.3

threats.title: Data Poisoning via Head-End
threats.description:
This includes False Data Injection, command injection and other tampering attacks via the Head-End system.
 This threat is only partially mitigated as the BSI does not require the use of cross-validation. It is recommended, but not required, leaving it up to the power provider, which in turn cannot be assumed to be in place. Anomaly detection is also not required, but recommended.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschtz and IT-SiG/KRITIS mandate anomaly detection and monitoring. In combination with IEC 62351, they require the use of API security gateways, strong authentication via MFA and input validation. They also mandate network segmentation and cryptographic intergrity checks (MACs). The BSI recommends cross-validation, but does not require it and can thus not be assumed to be inplace.
- API security gateways
- Strong authentication
- Input validation
- Monitoring
- Network segmentation
n- Cryptographic integrity checks (MACs)

threats.title: API Abuse
threats.description: An attacker exploits weak authentication, input validation or excessive privileges in exposed APIs to gain unauthorized access, inject malicious data or disrupt the grid operations.
threats.mitigation:
The API Abuse threat is slightly mitigated through the BSI-Grundschutz and the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation, which require API security gateways and rate limiting. With the addition of IEC 62351, it also requires anomaly monitoring, MFA, RBAC and input validation. This reduces the risk, but because of the fast advancement of API abuse attacks, this is still a valid attack vector.
- API security gateways
- Strong authentication (MFA)
- Input validation
- Monitoring
- Rate limiting
- RBAC

- Access control

threats.title: Fingerprinting
threats.description: Fingerprinting is an information diclosure attack, where an adversary sends specific requests to a system to gather information about its configuration, version, protocols, or internal structure. This allows an attacker to form other attacks and increase its successrate based on the gained information.
threats.mitigation:
This threat i partially mitigated by the BSI-Grundschtz and IEC 62351, which require the use of information disclosure and obfuscation, as well as IDS, TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable), regular patching and configuration managment and network segmentation. They also make sure that legacy protocols are disabled such as TLS 1.1 and earlier. The NISTR 7628 additionally requires the use of privacy-enhancing technologies, reducing system structure exposure. It is also recommended to do routine audits and system response testing.
- Restrict information disclosure (obfuscate certain information)
- Disable legacy protrocols and unnecessary services 
- Network segmentation
- IDS
- TLS 1.3

threats.title: DDoS
threats.description: Coordinated attacks against multiple communication nodes simultaneously using the denial of service attack or by utilizing multiple compromised systems to dirupt the communication.
threats.mitigation:
The main mitigating regulation for this threat is the BSI-Grundschutz, which mandates rate limiting & traffic filtering, as well es network segmentation and redundant network paths. It also requires DDoS mitigation services, which requires the operator to use state-of-the art countermeasures. These are all enforced by the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation. In combination with IEC 62351 they also require strong authentication via MFA and the use of the IDS, which is also required in the NIS 2.
- Rate limiting and traffic filtering
- Redundant network paths and failover
- DDoS mitigation appliances/services
- Network segmentation
- Strong authentication
- IDS

threats.title: Lateral Movement
threats.description: Attackers moving laterally across network boundaries
threats.mitigation:
For this threat the BSI-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS are the most important regulations. They guarantee that the network is segmentated (micro-segmentation), there is a zero-trust network policy in place and RBAC is present. THey also ensure intrusion detection and in combination with IEC 62351 strong authentication (MFA).
- Zero-trust network architecture
- Micro-segmentation
- least-privilege access
- Strong authentication with MFA
- Intrusion detection

threats.title: Eavesdropping
threats.description:
Passive monitoring of sensitive operational data and control commands 
 This one is mitigated through TLS 1.2/3 and IEC 62351 which provide strong encryption for data in transit.
threats.mitigation:
For this threat the most important regulations are IEC 62351 and the BSI-Grundschutz, which mandate the use of strong encryption TLS 1.2 (vulnerable to subversion attacks) and IPsec. Additionally, they require regular key rotation and management, cryptographic message syntax (CMS), IDS, networksegmentation and aduti and logging. It is also required to use strong authentication (MFA), endpiont security and hardening and secure time synchronization.
- Use strong encryption (TLS 1.3/IPsec) for all data in transit
- Regular key rotation and management
 - Use CMS
 - IDPS
 - Network Segmentation
 - Secure Time Synchronization

threats.title: Credential Compromise
threats.description: Stolen authentication credentials being used to traverse trust boundaries
threats.mitigation:
The IEC 62351 regualtion mandates the use of strong authentication via MFA and RBAC with least privilege rules, as well as autid trails and behavioral monitoring. It also ensures the use of strong encryption via TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable), user and device identity lifecycle management and regular credential and password management. All tis is further enforced by the BSI-Grundschutz and the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation.
- Multi-factor authentication
- RBAC with least privilege
- Audit trails and behavioral monitoring

The BSI-Grundschutz also requires network segmentation and vendor managed software/code singing.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support

- Enforce certificate pinning

threats.title: DoS
threats.description: Flooding critical communication channels, especially affecting real-time control systems
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation mandates rate limiting and redundant network paths with failover and network segmentation. It also requires IDS, which includes traffic anomaly analytics and network behavior baselines. They also require DDoS mitiagtion services, which force the operator to use state-of-the art countermeasures. It is also required to push incident reports and use collaborative filtering and threat intelligence sharing.
- Rate limiting and traffic filtering
- Redundant network paths and failover
- DDoS mitigation appliances/services

threats.title: Backdoored Firmware

threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 requires the use of secure boot, code signing and verification, as well es limited access priviledges, which is enforces by the BSI-Grundschutz. This also mandates with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation to utilize vendor risk assesment, zero trust supply chain model and regular patching and updating.
- Vendor Risk Assesment
- Code Signing & Verification (CMS)
- Secure Boot
- Ongoing Code Scanning
- Zero Trust Supply Chain Model
- Limited Access Privileges
- Regular Patching/Updating

data.name: GOOSE-Trip
data.description: A GOOSE-Trip is a high-priority signal sent to a Protected-IED instructing it to respond to a detected fault by activating a circuit breaker or other field device. It is designed for ultra-fast execution, typically within less than 4 milliseconds, ensuring rapid fault isolation and system protection.

threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 require the use of secure and up-to-date protocols, however their requirements are not updated frequently enough, such that IEC 62351 for example only requires TLS 1.2 and recommends TLS 1.3 instead of requiring TLS 1.3 to prevent subversion attacks. Otherwise, the two regulations mandate the disabling of legacy/weak protocols, the use of MACs and digital signatures such as HMAC, strong authentication through MFA and continuous monitoring. The BSI-Grundschutz also requires network segmentation and vendor managed software/code singing.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support

In combination with IEC 62351 they also require strong authentication via MFA and the use of the IDS, which is also required in the NIS 2.

threats.title: Insider Privilege Abuse
threats.description: An insider (authorized user like an empoyee, contractor or third party) can push an update in order to insert either a backdoor or grand privileges to unauthorized people. They can also take control of the network for malicious purposes or personal gain.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351, which require the use of RBAC and at least TLS 1.2. Furthermore, the BSI-Grundschutz in combination with the SiG/KRITIS regulation require the implementation of behavioral monitoring, seperation of duties and vendor vetting.

- Secure remote access
- Seperation of Duties

- Vendor and contractor vetting

threats.title: API Abuse
threats.description: An attacker exploits weak authentication, input validation or excessive privileges in exposed APIs to gain unauthorized access, inject malicious data or disrupt the grid operations.
threats.mitigation:
The API Abuse threat is slightly mitigated through the BSI-Grundschutz and the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation, which require API security gateways and rate limiting. With the addition of IEC 62351, it also requires anomaly monitoring, MFA, RBAC and input validation. This reduces the risk, but because of the fast advancement of API abuse attacks, this is still a valid attack vector.
- API security gateways
- Strong authentication (MFA)
- Input validation
- Monitoring
- Rate limiting
- RBAC

threats.title: Rogue Device Injection
threats.description: Entering a third party device that porpusfully inserts false data or ignores control commands. Furthermore, it can impersonate legitimite equipment, collect sensitive data or be a foothold for further attacks.
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 requires strong authenticaion and communication via TLS 1.2, leaving room for subversion attacks. While the BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation network segmentation, RBAC and privacy-preserving data aggregation regquire. Note that differential privacy is not mandated, so there is room for improvment and a possible attack vector. TR-30109 for SMGW and the BSI-Grundschutz also require CRL.
- Strong authentication (TLS 1.3) 
- Network segmentation
- Privacy-preserving data aggregation
- Role-based access control
- CRL

threats.title: Replay Attack
threats.description: Replaying legitimate control commands at inappropriate times
threats.mitigation:
The threat is mostly mitigated by IEC 62351, which ensures TLS 1.2 (subversion attack vulnerable), nonce/timestamp usage, authenticated time sources, MACs, and sequence number checking. In combination with the BSI-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation it also requires redundant time synchronization, anomaly detection and monitoring, file/session integrity validation and network segmentation.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Use nonces/timestamps to prevent replay
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support
- Network Segmentation
- Anomaly Detection

threats.title: Corrupting Telemetry

threats.mitigation:
The threat is partially mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 and IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation, which require network segmentation, strict firewall rules, cryptographic integrity checks (CMS, HMACs) and intrusion detection. They also mandate secure protocol gateways and secure time synchronization, but due to the complexity of the telemetry systems tampering remains realistic.
- Network segmentation between it and ot
- Strict firewall rules
- Secure protocol gateways
- Cryptographic integrity (CMS, HMAC)
- IDS
- Secure time sync (IEE 1588 PTP)

threats.title: Timestamp Tampering
threats.description: Affecting coordination between protection systems and control centers by altering or manipulating timestamps asccociated with data, commands or logs. This is done to either bypass security controls, hide malicious activity or disrupt synchronization and sequencing in critical systems.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz. Incombination with IEC 61850 it requires authenticated time sources, redundant time synchronization mechanisms and secure time synchronization protocos. In combincation with IEC 62351 it also mandates monitoring of anomalies in time data, file/digital integrity checks and strong authentication. TLS 1.2 for all communiations is also mandated, but leafs room for subversion attacks.
- Use authenticated time sources
- Redundant time synchronization mechanisms
- Monitor for anomalies
- Secure time synchronization protocols (network time protocol (NTP) with authentication or precision time protocol (PTP))
- TLS 1.3
- Strong authentication
- File integrity checks

IEC 62351 with the BSI-Grundschutz mandates the use of secure and up-to-date protocols, disabling legacy/weak protocols and protocol version pinning. Furthermore requires the use of nonces/timestamps to prevent replay and the BSI-Grundschutz requires network segmentation.

- Network segmentation
- Protocol version pinning

threats.title: Data Poisoning via Head-End
threats.description:
This includes False Data Injection, command injection and other tampering attacks via the Head-End system.
 This threat is only partially mitigated as the BSI does not require the use of cross-validation. It is recommended, but not required, leaving it up to the power provider, which in turn cannot be assumed to be in place. Anomaly detection is also not required, but recommended.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschtz and IT-SiG/KRITIS mandate anomaly detection and monitoring. In combination with IEC 62351, they require the use of API security gateways, strong authentication via MFA and input validation. They also mandate network segmentation and cryptographic intergrity checks (MACs). The BSI recommends cross-validation, but does not require it and can thus not be assumed to be inplace.

- Strong authentication
- Input validation
- Monitoring
- Network segmentation

This allows an attacker to form other attacks and increase its successrate based on the gained information.
threats.mitigation:
This threat i partially mitigated by the BSI-Grundschtz and IEC 62351, which require the use of information disclosure and obfuscation, as well as IDS, TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable), regular patching and configuration managment and network segmentation. They also make sure that legacy protocols are disabled such as TLS 1.1 and earlier. The NISTR 7628 additionally requires the use of privacy-enhancing technologies, reducing system structure exposure. It is also recommended to do routine audits and system response testing.
- Restrict information disclosure (obfuscate certain information)
- Disable legacy protrocols and unnecessary services 
- Network segmentation
- IDS
- TLS 1.3

This includes False Data Injection, command injection and other tampering attacks via the Head-End system.
 This threat is only partially mitigated as the BSI does not require the use of cross-validation. It is recommended, but not required, leaving it up to the power provider, which in turn cannot be assumed to be in place. Anomaly detection is also not required, but recommended.
threats.mitigation:

- Input validation
- Monitoring
- Network segmentation
n- Cryptographic integrity checks (MACs)

In combination with IEC 62351 they also require strong authentication via MFA and the use of the IDS, which is also required in the NIS 2.

This allows an attacker to form other attacks and increase its successrate based on the gained information.

It is also recommended to do routine audits and system response testing.

- Disable legacy protrocols and unnecessary services 
- Network segmentation
- IDS
- TLS 1.3

threats.mitigation:

The physical security controls further reduce the risk of this threat, where strong authentication and authorization (MFA, RBAC) and encrypted communication of TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable) help. Network segmentation, intrusion detection and suply chain security are also mandated by the three regulations and reduce the likelyhood of this threat to rate (2).

- Regular vulnerability scanning and patch management
- Physical security controls

data.name: Set Charging Profile
data.description: A Set Charging Profile message involves setting the electric vehicle (EV) charging or discharging schedule, which includes V2G setpoints, power limits, and time windows. It uses the ISO 15118-20 'ScheduleExchange' protocol over a secure TLS 1.2 (or higher) connection to communicate these plans between the EV and charging infrastructure. This enables precise control of charging and discharging profiles for optimized grid interaction and EV energy management. The ScheduleExchange message specifically allows adjusting the charging schedule dynamically, including parameters like departure time, target energy, and maximum power points, ensuring coordinated and flexible charge management in compliance with ISO 15118-20 standards.

threats.title: DDoS
threats.description: Coordinated attacks against multiple communication nodes simultaneously using the denial of service attack or by utilizing multiple compromised systems to dirupt the communication.
threats.mitigation:
The main mitigating regulation for this threat is the BSI-Grundschutz, which mandates rate limiting & traffic filtering, as well es network segmentation and redundant network paths. It also requires DDoS mitigation services, which requires the operator to use state-of-the art countermeasures. These are all enforced by the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation. In combination with IEC 62351 they also require strong authentication via MFA and the use of the IDS, which is also required in the NIS 2.
- Rate limiting and traffic filtering
- Redundant network paths and failover
- DDoS mitigation appliances/services
- Network segmentation
- Strong authentication
- IDS

threats.title: Command Injection
threats.description: Inserting malicious control commands into legitimate communication streams
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 is the most important regulation with the BSI-Grundschutz, which require MFA, RBAC, input validation, TLS 1.2 (subversion attack vulnerable) and secure boot. IEC 62351 also required parameterized commands to prevent malicious free-from commands.

- Role-based access control (RBAC)
- Input validation and whitelisting
- TLS 1.3 and ceritificate pinning (IEC 62351-3)
- Parameterized Commands (structured data formats)
- Secure Boot

threats.title: Replay Attack
threats.description: Replaying legitimate control commands at inappropriate times
threats.mitigation:
The threat is mostly mitigated by IEC 62351, which ensures TLS 1.2 (subversion attack vulnerable), nonce/timestamp usage, authenticated time sources, MACs, and sequence number checking. In combination with the BSI-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation it also requires redundant time synchronization, anomaly detection and monitoring, file/session integrity validation and network segmentation.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Use nonces/timestamps to prevent replay
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support
- Network Segmentation
- Anomaly Detection

threats.title: Microgrid Compromise
threats.description: Manipulation of local energy management and control systems through unauthorized access to the devices or their communication.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 and IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation require secure boot, physical sercurity controls and network segmentation with firewalls and IDS. They also require regular vulnerability scanndin and patch management, as well as strong authentication (MFA) and RBAC. Logging and continuous monitoring via SIEM is also mandated and supply chain security and incident reports are also part of those regulations. All of this mostly mitigates this threat.
- Secure boot
- Regular vulnerability scanning and patch management
- Physical security controls
- Network Segmentation and firewalls with IDS

threats.title: Load Manipulation
threats.description: The attacker compromises or coordinates high-wattage controllable loads to destablilize the grid, cause frequency/voltage excursions or disrupt market operations.
threats.mitigation:
Similar to other tampering threats this threat is partially mitigated by the IEC 62351 and IT-SiG/KRITIS regualtion as well as the BSI-Grundschutz. They require strong authentication, network segmentation, network path redundancy and IDS. They also mandate strong encryption via TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable) and recommend regurious cross validation. Fast load shedding is also required by the VDE-AR-N 4142.
- Strong authentication
- Network segmentation
- Anomaly detection or IDS
- Redundancy
- Strong encryption and signature (TLS 1.3, CMS)
- Fast Loadshedding

threats.title: Exploit vulnerable Protocols
threats.description: Exploiting vulnerabilities in DNP3, IEC 61850, and other industrial protocols
threats.mitigation:
Similar to the legacy protocol threat, this threat is mostyl mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and the IEC 62351 regulation, which enforce up-to-date protocols (some exceptions, such as TLS 1.2) and disable legacy protocols. Furthermore they require the use of HMAC, digital signatures and CMS for integrity checks, mutual strong authentication through MFA and network segmentation. Netwrok segmentation in combination with continuous monitoring and anomaly detection do not prevent the threat, but reduce the risk for a significant impact.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support

threats.title: Unauthorized Voltage Setpoint Change
threats.description: Adversaries manipulate voltage setpoints in real-time via compromised micro-controllers, causing equipment damage or cascading outages.
threats.mitigation:
With the BSI-Grundschutz and the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation in use, it is required to use RBAC, anomaly detection via IDS and to have redundancy and fallback in place. With the IEC 62351 it is mandated to use TLS 1.2 for communications (subverion attack vulnerable), command validation (also IEC 61850), disable legacy protocols and use strong authentication (MFA).

- Strong Authentication (MFA)
- Command Validation
- Anomaly detection (IDS)
- End-to-End encryption (TLS 1.3)
- Redundancy and fallback
- Disable legacy protocols

threats.title: Fingerprinting
threats.description: Fingerprinting is an information diclosure attack, where an adversary sends specific requests to a system to gather information about its configuration, version, protocols, or internal structure. This allows an attacker to form other attacks and increase its successrate based on the gained information.
threats.mitigation:
This threat i partially mitigated by the BSI-Grundschtz and IEC 62351, which require the use of information disclosure and obfuscation, as well as IDS, TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable), regular patching and configuration managment and network segmentation. They also make sure that legacy protocols are disabled such as TLS 1.1 and earlier. The NISTR 7628 additionally requires the use of privacy-enhancing technologies, reducing system structure exposure. It is also recommended to do routine audits and system response testing.
- Restrict information disclosure (obfuscate certain information)
- Disable legacy protrocols and unnecessary services 
- Network segmentation
- IDS
- TLS 1.3

threats.title: Pricing Signal Manipulation
threats.description: Adversaries falsify dynamic pricing signals sent to DERs or consumers, triggering abrupt load changes that destabilize the grid or manipulate energy markets.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 regulation mandate the use of digital signatures for all pricing and load signals, as well as pricing validation via redundant sources and anomaly detection. They furthermore require strong authentication via MFA for all parties, secure communication via TLS 1.2 (vulnerable to subversion attacks), incident reporting with IDS and regular security assesments and penentration testing.
- Digitally sign all pricing/load signals (e.g., using IEC 62351 or OpenADR 3.0).
- Validate signals against redundant sources (e.g., independent market feeds).
- Deploy anomaly detection for abnormal price/load patterns.

Note that differential privacy is not mandated, so there is room for improvment and a possible attack vector. TR-30109 for SMGW and the BSI-Grundschutz also require CRL.
- Strong authentication (TLS 1.3) 
- Network segmentation
- Privacy-preserving data aggregation
- Role-based access control
- CRL

threats.title: Fingerprinting
threats.description: Fingerprinting is an information diclosure attack, where an adversary sends specific requests to a system to gather information about its configuration, version, protocols, or internal structure. This allows an attacker to form other attacks and increase its successrate based on the gained information.
threats.mitigation:
This threat i partially mitigated by the BSI-Grundschtz and IEC 62351, which require the use of information disclosure and obfuscation, as well as IDS, TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable), regular patching and configuration managment and network segmentation. They also make sure that legacy protocols are disabled such as TLS 1.1 and earlier. The NISTR 7628 additionally requires the use of privacy-enhancing technologies, reducing system structure exposure. It is also recommended to do routine audits and system response testing.
- Restrict information disclosure (obfuscate certain information)
- Disable legacy protrocols and unnecessary services 
- Network segmentation
- IDS
- TLS 1.3

threats.title: Corrupting Telemetry
threats.description: Corrupting telemetry data affecting grid monitoring and control decisions, note that it includes non-malicious causes, which are not included in fdi.
threats.mitigation:
The threat is partially mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 and IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation, which require network segmentation, strict firewall rules, cryptographic integrity checks (CMS, HMACs) and intrusion detection. They also mandate secure protocol gateways and secure time synchronization, but due to the complexity of the telemetry systems tampering remains realistic.
- Network segmentation between it and ot
- Strict firewall rules
- Secure protocol gateways
- Cryptographic integrity (CMS, HMAC)
- IDS
- Secure time sync (IEE 1588 PTP)

threats.title: Backdoored Firmware
threats.description: Backdoored firmware in field devices (e.g., smart meters or protection relays) enables persistent access.
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 requires the use of secure boot, code signing and verification, as well es limited access priviledges, which is enforces by the BSI-Grundschutz. This also mandates with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation to utilize vendor risk assesment, zero trust supply chain model and regular patching and updating.
- Vendor Risk Assesment
- Code Signing & Verification (CMS)
- Secure Boot
- Ongoing Code Scanning
- Zero Trust Supply Chain Model
- Limited Access Privileges
- Regular Patching/Updating

threats.title: Eavesdropping
threats.description:
Passive monitoring of sensitive operational data and control commands 
 This one is mitigated through TLS 1.2/3 and IEC 62351 which provide strong encryption for data in transit.
threats.mitigation:
For this threat the most important regulations are IEC 62351 and the BSI-Grundschutz, which mandate the use of strong encryption TLS 1.2 (vulnerable to subversion attacks) and IPsec. Additionally, they require regular key rotation and management, cryptographic message syntax (CMS), IDS, networksegmentation and aduti and logging. It is also required to use strong authentication (MFA), endpiont security and hardening and secure time synchronization.
- Use strong encryption (TLS 1.3/IPsec) for all data in transit
- Regular key rotation and management
 - Use CMS
 - IDPS
 - Network Segmentation
 - Secure Time Synchronization

data.name: Meter Data/Status
data.protocol: DLMS/COSEM over TLS 1.3, IEC 60870-5-104 with IEC 62351, HTTPS/TLS 1.3 (REST/JSON)

threats.title: API Abuse
threats.description: An attacker exploits weak authentication, input validation or excessive privileges in exposed APIs to gain unauthorized access, inject malicious data or disrupt the grid operations.
threats.mitigation:
The API Abuse threat is slightly mitigated through the BSI-Grundschutz and the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation, which require API security gateways and rate limiting. With the addition of IEC 62351, it also requires anomaly monitoring, MFA, RBAC and input validation. This reduces the risk, but because of the fast advancement of API abuse attacks, this is still a valid attack vector.
- API security gateways
- Strong authentication (MFA)
- Input validation
- Monitoring
- Rate limiting
- RBAC

data.name: Aggregated Data/Commands

It also includes command messages sent by the VPP Operator through the Head-End System, as well as alert events, enabling coordinated control and real-time monitoring of distributed resources.

threats.title: Lateral Movement
threats.description: Attackers moving laterally across network boundaries
threats.mitigation:
For this threat the BSI-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS are the most important regulations. They guarantee that the network is segmentated (micro-segmentation), there is a zero-trust network policy in place and RBAC is present. THey also ensure intrusion detection and in combination with IEC 62351 strong authentication (MFA).
- Zero-trust network architecture
- Micro-segmentation
- least-privilege access
- Strong authentication with MFA
- Intrusion detection

threats.title: Timestamp Tampering
threats.description: Affecting coordination between protection systems and control centers by altering or manipulating timestamps asccociated with data, commands or logs. This is done to either bypass security controls, hide malicious activity or disrupt synchronization and sequencing in critical systems.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz. Incombination with IEC 61850 it requires authenticated time sources, redundant time synchronization mechanisms and secure time synchronization protocos. In combincation with IEC 62351 it also mandates monitoring of anomalies in time data, file/digital integrity checks and strong authentication. TLS 1.2 for all communiations is also mandated, but leafs room for subversion attacks.
- Use authenticated time sources
- Redundant time synchronization mechanisms
- Monitor for anomalies
- Secure time synchronization protocols (network time protocol (NTP) with authentication or precision time protocol (PTP))
- TLS 1.3
- Strong authentication
- File integrity checks

threats.title: Load Manipulation
threats.description: The attacker compromises or coordinates high-wattage controllable loads to destablilize the grid, cause frequency/voltage excursions or disrupt market operations.
threats.mitigation:
Similar to other tampering threats this threat is partially mitigated by the IEC 62351 and IT-SiG/KRITIS regualtion as well as the BSI-Grundschutz. They require strong authentication, network segmentation, network path redundancy and IDS. They also mandate strong encryption via TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable) and recommend regurious cross validation. Fast load shedding is also required by the VDE-AR-N 4142.
- Strong authentication
- Network segmentation
- Anomaly detection or IDS
- Redundancy
- Strong encryption and signature (TLS 1.3, CMS)
- Fast Loadshedding

threats.title: Data Poisoning via Head-End
threats.description:
This includes False Data Injection, command injection and other tampering attacks via the Head-End system.
 This threat is only partially mitigated as the BSI does not require the use of cross-validation. It is recommended, but not required, leaving it up to the power provider, which in turn cannot be assumed to be in place. Anomaly detection is also not required, but recommended.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschtz and IT-SiG/KRITIS mandate anomaly detection and monitoring. In combination with IEC 62351, they require the use of API security gateways, strong authentication via MFA and input validation. They also mandate network segmentation and cryptographic intergrity checks (MACs). The BSI recommends cross-validation, but does not require it and can thus not be assumed to be inplace.
- API security gateways
- Strong authentication
- Input validation
- Monitoring
- Network segmentation
n- Cryptographic integrity checks (MACs)

data.name: Firmware/Software Update
data.description: Firmware/Software Update refers to the remote deployment of new firmware or software to system devices, including microgrid controllers, customer DER/IoT devices, PMUs, and other field equipment. This process ensures that all targeted devices receive the latest features, security patches, and performance improvements, enabling consistent functionality, enhanced cybersecurity, and minimal downtime across the network.

threats.title: Insider Privilege Abuse
threats.description: An insider (authorized user like an empoyee, contractor or third party) can push an update in order to insert either a backdoor or grand privileges to unauthorized people. They can also take control of the network for malicious purposes or personal gain.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351, which require the use of RBAC and at least TLS 1.2. Furthermore, the BSI-Grundschutz in combination with the SiG/KRITIS regulation require the implementation of behavioral monitoring, seperation of duties and vendor vetting.
- Least privilege
- Behavioral monitoring
- Strict contractor vetting
- Secure remote access
- Seperation of Duties
- Strong authentication
- Logging and audit trials
- Vendor and contractor vetting
 RBAC

threats.title: DDoS
threats.description: Coordinated attacks against multiple communication nodes simultaneously using the denial of service attack or by utilizing multiple compromised systems to dirupt the communication.
threats.mitigation:
The main mitigating regulation for this threat is the BSI-Grundschutz, which mandates rate limiting & traffic filtering, as well es network segmentation and redundant network paths. It also requires DDoS mitigation services, which requires the operator to use state-of-the art countermeasures. These are all enforced by the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation. In combination with IEC 62351 they also require strong authentication via MFA and the use of the IDS, which is also required in the NIS 2.
- Rate limiting and traffic filtering
- Redundant network paths and failover
- DDoS mitigation appliances/services
- Network segmentation
- Strong authentication
- IDS

threats.title: Replay Attack
threats.description: Replaying legitimate control commands at inappropriate times
threats.mitigation:
The threat is mostly mitigated by IEC 62351, which ensures TLS 1.2 (subversion attack vulnerable), nonce/timestamp usage, authenticated time sources, MACs, and sequence number checking. In combination with the BSI-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation it also requires redundant time synchronization, anomaly detection and monitoring, file/session integrity validation and network segmentation.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Use nonces/timestamps to prevent replay
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support
- Network Segmentation
- Anomaly Detection

threats.title: DER-Gateway Exploitation
threats.description: Distributed Energy Resource control manipulation to destabilize grid operations
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS and IEC 62351 regulation mandate HSMs, secure boot, firmware integrity checks through difital signing, network segmentation and regualr vulnerability scanning and patch management. It is also required to use MFA, TLS 1.2 (vulnerable to subversion attacks), and IDS with incident reports.
- Secure boot
- Hardware security modules
- Firmware integrity checks
- Regular vulnerability scanning and patch management
- Network Segmentation

threats.title: Microgrid Compromise
threats.description: Manipulation of local energy management and control systems through unauthorized access to the devices or their communication.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 and IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation require secure boot, physical sercurity controls and network segmentation with firewalls and IDS. They also require regular vulnerability scanndin and patch management, as well as strong authentication (MFA) and RBAC. Logging and continuous monitoring via SIEM is also mandated and supply chain security and incident reports are also part of those regulations. All of this mostly mitigates this threat.
- Secure boot
- Regular vulnerability scanning and patch management
- Physical security controls
- Network Segmentation and firewalls with IDS

threats.title: Third Party Integration
threats.description: Vulnerabilities in external operator connections becoming entry points. They also open the door for backdoors and supply chain attacks.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation requires network segmentation, RBAC, continuous monitoring of inter-domain traffic and security vetting of third party components. In combination with IEC 62351 it requires strong authentication through MFA, secure software and firmware updates via code signing and encrypted communications through TLS 1.2.
- Network segmentation and strict inter-domain access controls
- Continuous monitoring of inter-domain traffic
- Security vetting of third-party components
- Strong authentication
- Secure Software and Firmware updates
- Information Sharing and Collaboration between power suppliers.

threats.mitigation:

They also require backups, regular patching, RBAC and endpoint protection and ISMS which is similar to EDR. IEC 62351 and the BSI-Grundschutz require strong authentication.

- Communication Monitoring
- Network Segmentation and zero trust policy
- Regular Patching

- Backup and Revocery (immutable backups)

Similar to the legacy protocol threat, this threat is mostyl mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and the IEC 62351 regulation, which enforce up-to-date protocols (some exceptions, such as TLS 1.2) and disable legacy protocols. Furthermore they require the use of HMAC, digital signatures and CMS for integrity checks, mutual strong authentication through MFA and network segmentation. Netwrok segmentation in combination with continuous monitoring and anomaly detection do not prevent the threat, but reduce the risk for a significant impact.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support

data.name: Aggregated meter readings
data.description: As the title suggests these are the aggregated meter readings, which are used for invoice calculation.

data.name: OCSP Response
data.description: An OCSP Response is the reply to an OCSP Request, providing the result of the certificate status check after appropriate analysis. It confirms whether the certificate is valid, revoked, or unknown. This ensures up-to-date and reliable verification for secure system communication.

threats.title: OCSP Responder Compromise
threats.description: Attackers can manipulate certificate validation responses, to allow them to revoke certificates to appear valid. This can even be done in real-time.
threats.mitigation:
This is mitigated by IEC 62351 which requires mutual authentication and TLS 1.2 for all PKI communications. In connection with the BSI-Grundschutz is also requires hardened and isolated PKI servers, regular audits, certificate pinning, RBAC and redundancy with integrity checks. With the addition of the TR-02102 regulation it also ensures regular key rotation.
 - Use of strong mutual TLS authentication for all PKI communications
- Harden and isolate PKI servers
- Regularly auditing and monitoring of certificate status and PKI integrity
- Certificate pinning and HSTS
 Regualr key rotation
 Strict access control
- Redundancy and integrity checks

threats.title: Exploit Vulnerable Protocols
threats.description: Exploiting vulnerabilities in DNP3, GOOSE over IEC 61850, MMS over IEC 61850, and IEC 61850-5-104
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 require the use of secure and up-to-date protocols, however their requirements are not updated frequently enough, such that IEC 62351 for example only requires TLS 1.2 and recommends TLS 1.3 instead of requiring TLS 1.3 to prevent subversion attacks. Otherwise, the two regulations mandate the disabling of legacy/weak protocols, the use of MACs and digital signatures such as HMAC, strong authentication through MFA and continuous monitoring. The BSI-Grundschutz also requires network segmentation and vendor managed software/code singing.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support

threats.title: Communication Protocol Downgrade
threats.description: Forcing systems to use weaker communication protocols, for example downgrade from TLS 1.3 to TLS 1.0.
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 with the BSI-Grundschutz mandates the use of secure and up-to-date protocols, disabling legacy/weak protocols and protocol version pinning. Furthermore requires the use of nonces/timestamps to prevent replay and the BSI-Grundschutz requires network segmentation.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Use nonces/timestamps to prevent replay
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support
- Network segmentation
- Protocol version pinning

- Strong authentication
- File integrity checks

- Network segmentation
- Privacy-preserving data aggregation
- Anomaly detection (IDS)
- Secure onboarding and lifecycle
- TLS 1.3

threats.title: Unauthorized Voltage Setpoint Change

threats.mitigation:
With the BSI-Grundschutz and the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation in use, it is required to use RBAC, anomaly detection via IDS and to have redundancy and fallback in place. With the IEC 62351 it is mandated to use TLS 1.2 for communications (subverion attack vulnerable), command validation (also IEC 61850), disable legacy protocols and use strong authentication (MFA).
- Role-based access control
- Strong Authentication (MFA)
- Command Validation
- Anomaly detection (IDS)
- End-to-End encryption (TLS 1.3)
- Redundancy and fallback
- Disable legacy protocols

threats.title: Command Injection

threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 is the most important regulation with the BSI-Grundschutz, which require MFA, RBAC, input validation, TLS 1.2 (subversion attack vulnerable) and secure boot. IEC 62351 also required parameterized commands to prevent malicious free-from commands.

- Role-based access control (RBAC)
- Input validation and whitelisting
- TLS 1.3 and ceritificate pinning (IEC 62351-3)
- Parameterized Commands (structured data formats)
- Secure Boot

They require strong authentication, network segmentation, network path redundancy and IDS. They also mandate strong encryption via TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable) and recommend regurious cross validation. Fast load shedding is also required by the VDE-AR-N 4142.

- Redundancy

- Fast Loadshedding

threats.description: Flooding critical communication channels, especially affecting real-time control systems
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation mandates rate limiting and redundant network paths with failover and network segmentation. It also requires IDS, which includes traffic anomaly analytics and network behavior baselines. They also require DDoS mitiagtion services, which force the operator to use state-of-the art countermeasures. It is also required to push incident reports and use collaborative filtering and threat intelligence sharing.

Similar to other communication threats this threat is partially mitigated through the BSI-Grundschutz, which enforces regular key rotation, network segmentation, strict usage of secure communication channels (TLS 1.2 & HTTPS [subverions attack vulnerable]) and with TR-03109 for SMGW requires certificate pinning. threats.title: Traffic Analysis

threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation and the IEC 62351 require the use of strong encryption (TLS 1.2, subversion vulnerable), as well as regular key rotation, network segmentation and access control & monitoring. They also require secure tunneling/VPN and the BSI-Grundschutz mandates security audits and reviews. The BSI guidance also requires traffic flow obfuscation/padding, reducing metadata leakage and inference risks.
- Use strong encryption (TLS/IPsec) for all data in transit
- Regular key rotation and management

threats.title: Exploit Vulnerable Protocols
threats.description: Exploiting vulnerabilities in DNP3, GOOSE over IEC 61850, MMS over IEC 61850, and IEC 61850-5-104
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 require the use of secure and up-to-date protocols, however their requirements are not updated frequently enough, such that IEC 62351 for example only requires TLS 1.2 and recommends TLS 1.3 instead of requiring TLS 1.3 to prevent subversion attacks. Otherwise, the two regulations mandate the disabling of legacy/weak protocols, the use of MACs and digital signatures such as HMAC, strong authentication through MFA and continuous monitoring. The BSI-Grundschutz also requires network segmentation and vendor managed software/code singing.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support

threats.title: Lateral Movement

threats.mitigation:
For this threat the BSI-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS are the most important regulations. They guarantee that the network is segmentated (micro-segmentation), there is a zero-trust network policy in place and RBAC is present. THey also ensure intrusion detection and in combination with IEC 62351 strong authentication (MFA).
- Zero-trust network architecture
- Micro-segmentation
- least-privilege access
- Strong authentication with MFA
- Intrusion detection

Passive monitoring of sensitive operational data and control commands 
 This one is mitigated through TLS 1.2/3 and IEC 62351 which provide strong encryption for data in transit.

It is also required to use strong authentication (MFA), endpiont security and hardening and secure time synchronization.
- Use strong encryption (TLS 1.3/IPsec) for all data in transit
- Regular key rotation and management
 - Use CMS
 - IDPS
 - Network Segmentation
 - Secure Time Synchronization

threats.title: Blind Security Monitoring

threats.mitigation:
With the BSI-Grundschutz, IT-SiG/KRITIS IEC 62351 regulation this threat is partially mitigated. They require secure log storage, redundant monitoring and IDS and anomaly detection with regular log audits. They also enforce a strong access control and authentication ruling with RBAC and MFA and network segmentation. It is also mandated to do continuous monitoring and incident reporting, but logs are still falsifyable and vulnerable to store now decrypt later attacks becuase of the missing quuantum security requirements.
- Secure log storage (write-once, cryptographically signed)
- Deploy redundant monitoring
- Use anomaly detection and regular log audits

In combination with IEC 62351 they also require strong authentication via MFA and the use of the IDS, which is also required in the NIS 2.

- Redundant network paths and failover

- Strong authentication
- IDS

- Use anomaly detection and regular log audits

threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation mandates rate limiting and redundant network paths with failover and network segmentation. It is also required to push incident reports and use collaborative filtering and threat intelligence sharing.
- Rate limiting and traffic filtering
- Redundant network paths and failover
- DDoS mitigation appliances/services

threats.title: Fingerprinting
threats.description: Fingerprinting is an information diclosure attack, where an adversary sends specific requests to a system to gather information about its configuration, version, protocols, or internal structure. This allows an attacker to form other attacks and increase its successrate based on the gained information.
threats.mitigation:
This threat i partially mitigated by the BSI-Grundschtz and IEC 62351, which require the use of information disclosure and obfuscation, as well as IDS, TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable), regular patching and configuration managment and network segmentation. They also make sure that legacy protocols are disabled such as TLS 1.1 and earlier. The NISTR 7628 additionally requires the use of privacy-enhancing technologies, reducing system structure exposure. It is also recommended to do routine audits and system response testing.
- Restrict information disclosure (obfuscate certain information)
- Disable legacy protrocols and unnecessary services 
- Network segmentation
- IDS
- TLS 1.3

It is also required to use strong authentication (MFA), endpiont security and hardening and secure time synchronization.

data.name: CRL push/download
data.description: A CRL push/download process involves the OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol) Responder sending a push notification to the CRL (Certificate Revocation List) server whenever a certificate is revoked. The OCSP Responder can also query the CRL server to respond to requests about the revocation status of a certificate and may cache and update the current version of the CRL as needed. This mechanism ensures timely distribution and synchronization of certificate revocation information, supporting fast and reliable validation of certificates in the system.

threats.mitigation:
Similar to the legacy protocol threat, this threat is mostyl mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and the IEC 62351 regulation, which enforce up-to-date protocols (some exceptions, such as TLS 1.2) and disable legacy protocols. This is done to either bypass security controls, hide malicious activity or disrupt synchronization and sequencing in critical systems.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz. Incombination with IEC 61850 it requires authenticated time sources, redundant time synchronization mechanisms and secure time synchronization protocos. In combincation with IEC 62351 it also mandates monitoring of anomalies in time data, file/digital integrity checks and strong authentication. TLS 1.2 for all communiations is also mandated, but leafs room for subversion attacks.
- Use authenticated time sources
- Redundant time synchronization mechanisms
- Monitor for anomalies
- Secure time synchronization protocols (network time protocol (NTP) with authentication or precision time protocol (PTP))
- TLS 1.3
- Strong authentication
- File integrity checks

data.name: Control Message
data.description: Control Messages include tariff changes, configuration data, and load reduction, shedding, or increase commands. They also carry configuration settings for the gateway, enabling dynamic adjustment of grid operation and device behavior. Note that they are broader than control commands, as they carry additional information.

threats.title: Communication Protocol Downgrade
threats.description: Forcing systems to use weaker communication protocols, for example downgrade from TLS 1.3 to TLS 1.0.
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 with the BSI-Grundschutz mandates the use of secure and up-to-date protocols, disabling legacy/weak protocols and protocol version pinning. Furthermore requires the use of nonces/timestamps to prevent replay and the BSI-Grundschutz requires network segmentation.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Use nonces/timestamps to prevent replay
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support
- Network segmentation
- Protocol version pinning

threats.title: Timestamp Tampering
threats.description: Affecting coordination between protection systems and control centers by altering or manipulating timestamps asccociated with data, commands or logs. This is done to either bypass security controls, hide malicious activity or disrupt synchronization and sequencing in critical systems.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz. Incombination with IEC 61850 it requires authenticated time sources, redundant time synchronization mechanisms and secure time synchronization protocos. In combincation with IEC 62351 it also mandates monitoring of anomalies in time data, file/digital integrity checks and strong authentication. TLS 1.2 for all communiations is also mandated, but leafs room for subversion attacks.
- Use authenticated time sources
- Redundant time synchronization mechanisms
- Monitor for anomalies
- Secure time synchronization protocols (network time protocol (NTP) with authentication or precision time protocol (PTP))
- TLS 1.3
- Strong authentication
- File integrity checks

threats.title: DoS
threats.description: Flooding critical communication channels, especially affecting real-time control systems
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation mandates rate limiting and redundant network paths with failover and network segmentation. It also requires IDS, which includes traffic anomaly analytics and network behavior baselines. They also require DDoS mitiagtion services, which force the operator to use state-of-the art countermeasures. It is also required to push incident reports and use collaborative filtering and threat intelligence sharing.
- Rate limiting and traffic filtering
- Redundant network paths and failover
- DDoS mitigation appliances/services

threats.title: Man-in-the-Middle
threats.description: Intercepting certificate validation traffic to enable impersonation attacks
threats.mitigation:
Similar to other communication threats this threat is partially mitigated through the BSI-Grundschutz, which enforces regular key rotation, network segmentation, strict usage of secure communication channels (TLS 1.2 & HTTPS [subverions attack vulnerable]) and with TR-03109 for SMGW requires certificate pinning. Most of them are also enforces through the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation.
- Enforce certificate pinning
 - strict TLS validation
- Use HSTS and OCSP stapling
 - Regular Key Rotation
 Network segmentation

threats.title: Traffic Analysis
threats.description: Inferring operational patterns and vulnerabilities through network traffic observation
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation and the IEC 62351 require the use of strong encryption (TLS 1.2, subversion vulnerable), as well as regular key rotation, network segmentation and access control & monitoring. They also require secure tunneling/VPN and the BSI-Grundschutz mandates security audits and reviews. The BSI guidance also requires traffic flow obfuscation/padding, reducing metadata leakage and inference risks.
- Use strong encryption (TLS/IPsec) for all data in transit
- Regular key rotation and management

threats.title: Exploit Vulnerable Protocols
threats.description: Exploiting vulnerabilities in DNP3, GOOSE over IEC 61850, MMS over IEC 61850, and IEC 61850-5-104
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 require the use of secure and up-to-date protocols, however their requirements are not updated frequently enough, such that IEC 62351 for example only requires TLS 1.2 and recommends TLS 1.3 instead of requiring TLS 1.3 to prevent subversion attacks. Otherwise, the two regulations mandate the disabling of legacy/weak protocols, the use of MACs and digital signatures such as HMAC, strong authentication through MFA and continuous monitoring. The BSI-Grundschutz also requires network segmentation and vendor managed software/code singing.
- Only allow secure, up-to-date protocols (e.g., TLS 1.3)
- Disable legacy/weak protocol support

threats.title: Eavesdropping
threats.description:
Passive monitoring of sensitive operational data and control commands 
 This one is mitigated through TLS 1.2/3 and IEC 62351 which provide strong encryption for data in transit.
threats.mitigation:
For this threat the most important regulations are IEC 62351 and the BSI-Grundschutz, which mandate the use of strong encryption TLS 1.2 (vulnerable to subversion attacks) and IPsec. Additionally, they require regular key rotation and management, cryptographic message syntax (CMS), IDS, networksegmentation and aduti and logging. It is also required to use strong authentication (MFA), endpiont security and hardening and secure time synchronization.
- Use strong encryption (TLS 1.3/IPsec) for all data in transit
- Regular key rotation and management
 - Use CMS
 - IDPS
 - Network Segmentation
 - Secure Time Synchronization
