data.name: Field Device and Gateway Boundry
data.description: Trennt Field/edge Geraete von dem Kernnetzwerk und den IoT/IT Geraeten.

data.name: Maintenance/Update Boundary
data.description: Trennt remote maintenance/update server mit deren erhoehten Privilegien von den anderen Geraeten.

data.name: Customer Premise Boundary
data.description: Trennt Utility-managed Infrastruktur mit erhoehten Privilegien von Kunden-Geraeten und Netwerken.

data.name:
Outage 
Management 
System
data.description: An Outage Management System (OMS) polls Smart Meter Gateways using protocols like ANSI C12.22 and DLMS, analyzing their responses to detect power outages. It enables quick identification and localization of faults to support efficient restoration and improve grid reliability.

data.name: Operational Data Store
data.description: The ODS is a centralized repository that collects, stores, and manages real-time and historical operational data from various smart grid components. It supports monitoring, analysis, and decision-making processes by providing accurate and timely data on grid status, performance, and events. The integrity and availability of the ODS are critical, as tampering or compromise can lead to incorrect operational decisions, billing errors, and degraded grid reliability.

threats.title: Compromised Operational Data Store
threats.description: An attacker compromises the Data Store and tampers with the logged data leading to wrong conclusions from historical data and incorrect billing calculation.This is mitigated by TR-30109 requireing RBAC, while the BSI-Grundschutz requires network segmentation. Furthermore the IT-SiG and KRITIS regulation require log checking.
threats.mitigation:
This is mitigated by TR-30109 requireing RBAC, while the BSI-Grundschutz requires network segmentation. Furthermore the IT-SiG and KRITIS regulation require log checking. The BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351 also require strong mutual authentication via MFA and TLS 1.2 (subversion vulnerable), as well as hardening and isolating PKI servers. IT is also mandated to have physical security protocols, backup and recovery processes, access monitoring and anomaly detection and compreshensive audit trails and tampering-evident loggin in place. Integrity verification mechanisms via HMACs are also required.
- Use of strong mutual TLS authentication for all PKI communications
- Harden and isolate PKI servers
- Regularly auditing and monitoring of certificate status and PKI integrity

threats.title: Ransomeware
threats.description: Encrypting critical operational data and systems to lock out operators until a ransom is payed.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the use of IEC 62351 which requires TLS 1.2, leaving some room for subversion attacks. It also requires MFA. The BSI IT-Grundschutz also requires redundancy for critical infrastructure.
- Redundancy
- Communication Monitoring
- Network Segmentation and zero trust policy
- Regular Patching
- Strong authentication and access control
- Backup and Revocery (immutable backups)
- Endpoint Potection and Monitoring (EDR)

threats.title: Physical Threat
threats.description: The physical threat described the unauthorized access, tampering, vandalism, theft, sabotage, environmental hazards, or destruction of a device or infrastructure in the grid.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is partially mitigated, as physical destruction cannot be completely prevented. However, the BSI IT-Grundschutz and IT-SiG and KRITIS regulation require access control, access logging and physical tampering prevention through tampering-seals, keycards, RBAC and access logging.
- Permite Survailance 
- Tampering Seals
- Access Control (Keycards etc, Visitor/Maintanance Management)
- Access logging (digitally and physically)
- Lifecycle Security

data.name: IDS/SIEM
data.description: The Intrusion Detection System (IDS) monitors a network or system and issues an alert when it detects suspicious activities or violations of predefined rules. Security Information and Event Management (SIEM), in turn, collects log data for analysis and alerts the responsible units based on this data. Additionally, there is real-time system monitoring that informs the system administrator about relevant issues and creates event correlations. The two systems usually work together, where the IDS is responsible for the actual monitoring and detection of dangerous activities, and the SIEM is then alerted to initiate appropriate actions. The SIEM receives IDS alerts via Syslog, correlates security events, and prioritizes incidents for the Security Operation Center.

threats.title: Ransomeware
threats.description: Encrypting critical operational data and systems to lock out operators until a ransom is payed.
threats.mitigation:
Strong authentication and access control is required by IEC 62351 and regualr patching, which also requires TLS 1.2, leaving a hole for subversion attacks. Monitoring and logging is required by the BSI IT-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS regualtion.
- Redundancy
- Communication Monitoring
- Network Segmentation and zero trust policy
- Regular Patching
- Strong authentication and access control
- Backup and Revocery (immutable backups)
- Endpoint Potection and Monitoring (EDR)

data.name: CRL-Server
data.description: Provides Certificate Revocation Lists (CRLs) so that clients can check the validity of certificates.In essence, it is a type of data storage containing a list of all stored certificates that have been revoked by the OCSP responder. The CRL files themselves are signed by the Certificate Authority using RSA with SHA-256 or SHA-512.

threats.title: CRL-Server Compromise
threats.description: Certificate Revocation List tampering can enable the use of compromised certificates
threats.mitigation:
Mutual authentication and strong encryption is ensured through IEC 62351, network segmentation and monitoring is required by the BSI-Grundschutz and the SiG/KRITIS regulation. It is marked as partially mitigated as TLS 1.3 is not required and TLS 1.2 is vulnerable to subversion attacks.
- Use of strong mutual TLS authentication for all PKI communications
- Harden and isolate PKI servers
- Regularly auditing and monitoring of certificate status and PKI integrity

data.name: OCSP-Responder
data.description:
Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP): gibt in Echtzeit den Wiederrufstatus von X.509-Zertifikaten aus.
Im Allgemeinen ist es ein certificate manager der fuer die Erstellung, Speicherung und Wiederrufung digitaler Sicherheitszertifikate notwendig ist.

threats.title: OCSP Responder Compromise
threats.description: Attackers can manipulate certificate validation responses, to allow them to revoke certificates to appear valid. This can even be done in real-time.
threats.mitigation:
This is mitigated by IEC 62351 which requires mutual authentication and TLS 1.2 for all PKI communications. In connection with the BSI-Grundschutz is also requires hardened and isolated PKI servers, regular audits, certificate pinning, RBAC and redundancy with integrity checks. With the addition of the TR-02102 regulation it also ensures regular key rotation.
 - Use of strong mutual TLS authentication for all PKI communications
- Harden and isolate PKI servers
- Regularly auditing and monitoring of certificate status and PKI integrity
- Certificate pinning and HSTS
 Regualr key rotation
 Strict access control
- Redundancy and integrity checks

data.name: PMU
data.description: A Phasor Measurement Unit (PMU) measures phasor quantities such as voltage and current magnitude, frequency, and Rate of Change of Frequency (ROCOF) with precise time synchronization using the Precision Time Protocol (PTP). It streams this time-stamped data to Wide Area Monitoring Systems (WAMS) to enable real-time grid visualization, stability analysis, and fast detection of disturbances for enhanced grid reliability and control.

threats.title: GPS Spoofing
threats.description: Disrupting phasor measurement timing, causing false fault detection and potential line disconnections
threats.mitigation:
IEC 62351 requires the use of authenticated time sources, through the PTP protocol it also requires monitoring and anomaly detection and TLS 1.2 for every communication. In combincation with the BSI-Grundschutz is also requires redundant time synchronization mechanisms, mitigating this threat.
- Use authenticated time sources
- Redundant time synchronization mechanisms
- Monitor for anomalies in time data

threats.title: Physical Threat
threats.description: The physical threat described the unauthorized access, tampering, vandalism, theft, sabotage, environmental hazards, or destruction of a device or infrastructure in the grid.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz in combination with the SiG/KRITIS regulation, which requires permite survailance and physical protection such as tampering seals. RBAC is required by IEC 62351.- Permite Survailance 
- Tampering Seals
- Access Control (Keycards etc, Visitor/Maintanance Management)
- Access logging (digitally and physically)
- Lifecycle Security

data.name: WAMS-Backend
data.description: A WAMS-Backend (Wide Area Monitoring System) collects phasor data and functions as an analysis platform for wide-area monitoring and protection, including state estimation. It visualizes this data and prepares it for operators to enable informed decision-making and enhance grid stability across large regions.
data.privilegeLevel: Access to Sensitive Data, Critical Security Role, Full Operational Control

data.name: DSO-DMS
data.description: Distributed System Operator: Real-Time Grid Operation and Monitoring. It uses the Distribution Management System to issue control commands based on the InfoReports and other incoming information, in order to ensure system requirements are met. Distribution Management System: Network monitoring, load flow and voltage regulation, FLISR functions.

threats.title: Insider Privilege Abuse
threats.description: An insider (authorized user like an empoyee, contractor or third party) can push an update in order to insert either a backdoor or grand privileges to unauthorized people. They can also take control of the network for malicious purposes or personal gain.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351, which require the use of RBAC and at least TLS 1.2. Furthermore, the BSI-Grundschutz in combination with the SiG/KRITIS regulation require the implementation of behavioral monitoring, seperation of duties and vendor vetting.
- Least privilege
- Behavioral monitoring
- Strict contractor vetting
- Secure remote access
- Seperation of Duties
- Strong authentication
- Logging and audit trials
- Vendor and contractor vetting
 RBAC

threats.title: Privacy Leakage via Smart Meter Data
threats.description: A third party or otherwise compromised IoT device can leak information about the grid or customer information, leading to a privacy degredation.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and the IEC 62351 regualtion require device authentication, RBAC and use of IDS systems. General anonymization is also required, however, the anonymization algorithm which is only recommending differential privacy but now requireing it leafs room for privacy leakage.
- Device authentication 
- network segmentation
- privacy-preserving data aggregation
- TLS 1.3
- Anonymization (differential privacy) and aggregation
- Access control
- IDS

data.privilegeLevel: Full Operational Control, Access to Sensitive Data, Critical Security Role

data.name: Protected-IED
data.description: A Protected-IED (Intelligent Electronic Device) is a protection relay for distance and differential protection that detects faults and sends trip commands to breakers via GOOSE messaging to isolate faulty network zones. It also includes monitoring functions to support grid safety and rapid fault isolation, enhancing system reliability and operational awareness.

threats.title: Physical Threat
threats.description: The physical threat described the unauthorized access, tampering, vandalism, theft, sabotage, environmental hazards, or destruction of a device or infrastructure in the grid.
threats.mitigation:
Similar to other physical threats this is mitigated by the BSI-Grundschutz and IEC 62351, which require redundancy, permite survailance, access logging and RBAC.
- Permite Survailance 
- Tampering Seals
- Access Control (Keycards etc, Visitor/Maintanance Management)
- Access logging (digitally and physically)
- Lifecycle Security

data.privilegeLevel: Full Operational Control, Access to Sensitive Data, Critical Security Role

data.name: Operator UI
data.description: An Operator UI provides system operators with a clear, real-time overview of the entire power network, including voltage, current, power values, and status alerts, enabling them to monitor, control, and optimize grid operation effectively. It supports interaction via computers and mobile devices, facilitating quick responses to network conditions and ensuring secure, flexible management of distributed energy resources and grid components.

threats.title: Insider Privilege Abuse
threats.description: An insider (authorized user like an empoyee, contractor or third party) can push an update in order to insert either a backdoor or grand privileges to unauthorized people. They can also take control of the network for malicious purposes or personal gain.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the IT-SiG/KRITIS regualtion and the BSI-Grundschutz, which require least privilege implementation through RBAC, continuous monitoring, contractor vetting, seperation of duties and logging. The IEC 62351 mandates secure remote access and strong authentication.
- Least privilege
- Behavioral monitoring
- Strict contractor vetting
- Secure remote access
- Seperation of Duties
- Strong authentication
- Logging and audit trials
- Vendor and contractor vetting
 RBAC

threats.title: Social Engineering
threats.description: Targeting operational personnel with access to critical systems
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation require security trainings, multifactor authentication (also IEC 62351), RBAC and autig trails. However, because of the inherent human nature this threat cannot be considered more then partially mitigated.
- Security Trainings
- Quick reaction to security attacks
- Multi-factor authentication
- RBAC with least privilege
- Audit trails and behavioral monitoring

threats.title: API Abuse
threats.description: An attacker exploits weak authentication, input validation or excessive privileges in exposed APIs to gain unauthorized access, inject malicious data or disrupt the grid operations.
threats.mitigation:
The API Abuse threat is slightly mitigated through the BSI-Grundschutz and the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation, which require API security gateways and rate limiting. With the addition of IEC 62351, it also requires anomaly monitoring, MFA, RBAC and input validation. This reduces the risk, but because of the fast advancement of API abuse attacks, this is still a valid attack vector.
- API security gateways
- Strong authentication (MFA)
- Input validation
- Monitoring
- Rate limiting
- RBAC

threats.title: Privacy Leakage via Smart Meter Data
threats.description: A third party or otherwise compromised IoT device can leak information about the grid or customer information, leading to a privacy degredation. The severity could be higher, depending on the information that is communicated/collected.
threats.mitigation:
Partially mitigated as IEC 62351 requires TLS 1.2 which is vulnerable to subversion attacks and anonymization is not required to use differential privacy which is the only known anonymization mechanism to ensure privacy.
- Device authentication 
- network segmentation
- privacy-preserving data aggregation
- TLS 1.3
- Anonymization (differential privacy) and aggregation
- Access control
- IDS

threats.title: Fingerprinting
threats.description: Fingerprinting is an information diclosure attack, where an adversary sends specific requests to a system to gather information about its configuration, version, protocols, or internal structure. This allows an attacker to form other attacks and increase its successrate based on the gained information.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz mandates the obfuscation of system banners, error messages, version details and unnecessary information. It also prevents the usage of legacy protocols and ensures network segmentation. IEC 62351 forces the use of TLS 1.2, but not 1.3, which leafs the component vulnerable to subversion attacks.
- Restrict information disclosure (obfuscate certain information)
- Disable legacy protrocols and unnecessary services 
- Network segmentation
- IDS
- TLS 1.3

threats.title: Privileged Access Abuse
threats.description: Legitimate users with cross-boundary access conducting malicious activities
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz mandates network segmentation and RBAC. In combination with IEC 62351 is also enforces the use of MFA and with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation it requires audit trails and monitoring.
- Multi-factor authentication
- RBAC with least privilege
- Audit trails and behavioral monitoring

threats.title: Insider Privilege Abuse
threats.description: An insider (authorized user like an empoyee, contractor or third party) can push an update in order to insert either a backdoor or grand privileges to unauthorized people. They can also take control of the network for malicious purposes or personal gain.
threats.mitigation:
This threat is mitigated by the IT-SiG/KRITIS regualtion and the BSI-Grundschutz, which require least privilege implementation through RBAC, continuous monitoring, contractor vetting, seperation of duties and logging. The IEC 62351 mandates secure remote access and strong authentication.
- Least privilege
- Behavioral monitoring
- Strict contractor vetting
- Secure remote access
- Seperation of Duties
- Strong authentication
- Logging and audit trials
- Vendor and contractor vetting
 RBAC

data.name:
Customer 
DER/IoT
data.description: Distributed Energy Resources and Internet of Things devices located at the customer premises that interact with the smart grid. These include solar panels, home batteries, smart appliances, and IoT sensors, which can impact grid operations through energy generation, consumption, and control signals. Securing these devices is vital to prevent unauthorized manipulation affecting grid stability and reliability. (e.g., "Balcony PV," "Home Battery," "Smart Appliances")

threats.title: IoT Botnet Participation
threats.description: An IoT device is compromised and part of a botnet, which leads to ignored control commands, false data injection and/or control sequence injection. They can also be used to send spam, extend the botnet or perform a DoS or DDoS attack.
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz in combination with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation require network segmentation, IDS, secure onboarding and secure lifecycle management as well as privacy-preserving data aggregation. Note that the privacy requirements are not optimal. IEC 62351 also requires strong authentication and communication encryption TLS 1.2, which is vulnerable to subversion attacks.
- Device authentication 
- Network segmentation
- Privacy-preserving data aggregation
- Anomaly detection (IDS)
- Secure onboarding and lifecycle
- TLS 1.3

Forcasting 
System
data.description: A Forecasting System predicts energy generation, such as solar and wind power, and load patterns to support grid planning and operation. It enables proactive management by providing accurate short- and long-term forecasts that enhance grid stability and optimize resource allocation.

data.name: Billing System
data.description: Processes metering data for customer billing and settlement.
data.reasonOutOfScope: We do not have the capacity to also look at the billing process and the different evelope techniques that come with it.

data.name: Field Communication Infrastructure
data.description: Physical communication channels (e.g., fiber optics, power-line communication, wireless links) enabling data transmission between grid components. Vulnerable to physical tampering, environmental damage, and jamming

threats.title: Cable Tampering/Cutting
threats.description: Physical destruction of wired links (e.g., fiber, copper)
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz mandates physical security measures, which includes physical protection of infrastructure such as cables. The KRITIS regulation also enforces protection for critical infrastructure, such as physical detection sensors.
- Bury critical cables
- Use tamper-evident conduits
- Deploy seismic/vibration sensors

threats.title: Wireless Jamming
threats.description: Disrupting RF signals (e.g., LTE, Wi-Fi, Zigbee)
threats.mitigation:
While no explicit jamming mitigation standard exists, BSI IT-Grundschutz and KRITIS mandate availability and redundancy measures, which includes FHSS and fallback paths. They also mandata intrusion and anomaly detection, which helps with this threats.
- Frequency-hopping spread spectrum
- Wired fallback paths
- Jamming detection systems

threats.title: Device Tampering
threats.description: Unauthorized access to routers, switches, or repeaters
threats.mitigation:
Physical access control and tamper-evidence is required by BSI IT-Grundschutz. In combination with the IT-SiG/KRITIS regulation access logging and monitoring (including remote checks) is also required.
- Secure locked enclosures
- Tamper-evident seals
- Remote integrity checks

threats.title: Environmental Hazards
threats.description: Damage from floods, fires, or extreme weather
threats.mitigation:
The BSI-Grundschutz and IT-SiG/KRITIS mandate infrastructure resilience including protection against environmental risks, this includes redundancy and monitoring ensure availability and early warning of environmental damage.
- Weatherproof enclosures
- Redundant paths
- Environmental monitoring
